cards.grammarNote: أنا (ana) = I, used for both masculine and feminine speakers. In Arabic, the verb conjugation already encodes the subject, so أنا is often omitted in speech but used for emphasis.
Arabic distinguishes gender in 'you': أنتَ (anta) for masculine, أنتِ (anti) for feminine. The difference is a fatha (ـَ) vs kasra (ـِ) on the ت.
أنتِ (anti) = you (feminine). The kasra under the ت distinguishes it from أنتَ (anta = you, masculine). In unvowelled text they look identical — context and gender of the listener clarify.
هو (huwa) = he, and also 'it' for masculine nouns. Arabic has no neuter pronoun — every noun is masculine or feminine. الكتاب (the book) is masculine, so 'it' = هو.
هي (hiya) = she, and also 'it' for feminine nouns. الطاولة (the table) is feminine, so 'it' = هي.
نحن (nahnu) = we, used for both mixed and same-gender groups. The verb form for 'we' always begins with نـ (n-): نكتب (naktub = we write).
هم (hum) = they, for all-male or mixed-gender groups. The default plural pronoun. هن (hunna) is used for all-female groups.
Arabic has a special dual form for exactly two people or things. هما (huma) = the two of them. This is unique among major world languages. Dual also exists for nouns: كتابان (kitaban) = two books.
Equational sentence: no verb 'to be' in present tense. أنا (I) + طالب (student, masc.) = 'I am a student.' If the speaker is female: أنا طالبة (ana taaliba).
